topic: A person learns their college is closing down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The official notice, stark white against the worn laminate of my desk, felt like a physical assault. I reread the dates, the finality of the closure sinking into my chest like a lead weight. My jaw tightened until my molars ached. I slammed the paper down, the sound echoing too loudly in the suddenly cavernous office. "This is absurd," I muttered, the words tasting like ash. I stood up, pacing the cramped space, my movements jerky and sharp. Every corner of the room seemed to mock my helplessness. I kicked at a loose filing cabinet, the metal protesting under my force. I needed to scream, to shatter something, but all that escaped was a low, vibrating hum of pure, contained rage.

[story 2]

I stared at the eviction papers spread across my dorm room floor. The ink seemed to writhe under the harsh fluorescent light. My hands clenched into fists so tight my knuckles turned white, the tendons standing out like taut wires. I wanted to rip the documents to shreds, to watch the paper turn into confetti, but I hesitated, forcing myself to breathe through the constriction in my throat. "They can't just do this," I whispered, my voice dangerously low, laced with a brittle edge. I walked to the window, gripping the sill until my fingers ached, and looked out at the campus, a place that suddenly felt like a cage being dismantled by unseen hands. A hot flush spread across my neck, and I scrubbed at my face with the heel of my hand, trying to cool the internal inferno.

[story 3]

The meeting room felt suffocating. The dean’s carefully modulated voice detailing the budget cuts was a slow-motion train wreck of incompetence. I sat ramrod straight, my spine rigid, refusing to let my shoulders slump in defeat. When the representative finished speaking, I didn't offer a polite nod. Instead, I leaned forward, my voice cutting through the strained silence, sharp and unwavering. "Explain the rationale for this destruction," I demanded, my eyes locked on the speaker. I watched the subtle shift in their demeanor—a flicker of annoyance, a hint of defensiveness—and felt a surge of intense heat rise up my chest. My breath hitched, shallow and rapid. I wanted to shout down the entire assembly, to expose every flaw in their logic, but I held the sound back, letting the coiled tension in my body speak volumes instead.
